edges.description: Loss of ADK activity shifts adenosine away from AMP generation and raises intracellular and extracellular adenosine.
ADK deficiency has been reported with cervical arterial tortuosity and stroke, but the intervening vascular mechanism is unresolved.

ADK-deficient patients can show combined respiratory-chain complex defects in liver or skeletal muscle, producing a mitochondrial-disease-like energy-stress branch downstream of the primary purine and methionine metabolic defect.
Dysmorphic facial features are part of the recurrent syndromic phenotype, although the developmental intermediates connecting ADK loss to craniofacial findings are not established.
Cardiac abnormalities have been reported in ADK deficiency, but the path from the metabolic defect to cardiac development or function remains unresolved.
Short stature has been documented on long-term follow-up after liver transplantation in ADK deficiency, with unresolved causal intermediates.
Cortisol deficiency has been reported in long-term follow-up, but the connection between ADK loss and adrenal steroid physiology is unresolved.
Adenosine accumulation drives the S-adenosylhomocysteine hydrolase reaction backward and impairs methylation flux.
Elevated brain adenosine alters adenosine receptor signaling and synaptic plasticity, contributing to cognitive impairment and seizure risk.
The biochemical block is most evident in the liver and can present as neonatal or early infantile liver disease.
Methionine cycle disruption and excess adenosine also affect the central nervous system.
Disrupted methionine-cycle flux produces the characteristic elevated plasma methionine phenotype.
Disturbed transmethylation produces elevated S-adenosylhomocysteine, a diagnostically useful one-carbon metabolite abnormality.
Macrocytic anemia has been documented with the ADK transmethylation phenotype, including persistence after liver transplantation, but the causal intermediates remain unresolved.
Respiratory-chain defects in liver tissue are part of the mitochondrial-disease-like presentation accompanying ADK-related neonatal liver disease.
Hepatic involvement in ADK deficiency commonly presents as transient liver dysfunction with cholestasis.
Recurrent hypoglycemia occurs as part of the liver-centered metabolic phenotype in ADK deficiency.
Failure to thrive is reported in the same infantile multisystem metabolic presentation as hepatic dysfunction and recurrent hypoglycemia.
Neurodevelopmental impairment manifests clinically as global developmental delay.
The neurologic disease includes generalized hypotonia.
Brain ADK deficiency increases seizure susceptibility and epilepsy risk.
Delayed myelination is a reported neuroimaging manifestation in affected patients.
Cervical artery tortuosity and related cerebrovascular abnormalities can lead to stroke in a subset of patients.
Higher plasma methionine reflects the ADK-related methionine-cycle disturbance.
Elevated S-adenosylhomocysteine reflects disruption of the adenosine-sensitive transmethylation cycle.
Normal homocysteine is part of the diagnostic biochemical signature when interpreted together with elevated methionine.

evidence.snippet: ADK | HGNC:257 | adenosine kinase deficiency | MONDO:0100255 | AR | Definitive

nodes.description: Biallelic loss of ADK activity reduces phosphorylation of adenosine to AMP, impairing adenosine salvage and disturbing intracellular adenine nucleotide balance.

evidence.snippet: Adenosine kinase (ADK) deficiency is characterized by liver disease, dysmorphic features, epilepsy and developmental delay. This defect disrupts the adenosine/AMP futile cycle and interferes with the upstream methionine cycle.

nodes.description: Excess adenosine and reduced AMP availability alter purine homeostasis, disturb adenosine receptor signaling, and contribute to downstream neuronal and hepatic stress.

evidence.snippet: Pharmacological, biochemical, and electrophysiological studies suggest enhanced adenosine levels around synapses resulting in an enhanced adenosine A1 receptor (A1R)-dependent protective tone despite lower expression levels of the receptor.

nodes.description: Structural or other cardiac abnormalities have been reported in the human clinical phenotype.

evidence.snippet: It is characterized by developmental delay, hypotonia, epilepsy, facial dysmorphism, failure to thrive, transient liver dysfunction with cholestasis, recurrent hypoglycemia, and cardiac defects.

nodes.description: Vascular tortuosity of the cervical arteries and recurrent stroke have been reported as additional complications of ADK deficiency.

evidence.snippet: Here, we describe two patients with ADK deficiency and vascular tortuosity leading to stroke in one of them.
ADK deficiency is a rare inborn error of methionine metabolism with a complex phenotype that might be associated with cerebrovascular abnormalities and stroke.

nodes.description: Neonatal or early infantile cholestasis is a common presentation of the liver disease component.

evidence.snippet: transient liver dysfunction with cholestasis

nodes.description: Cortisol deficiency was documented during long-term follow-up in a patient with ADK deficiency.

evidence.snippet: Ten-year follow-up after LTx revealed a preserved good liver function, persistent regenerative macrocytic anemia, progressive neurological disease but disappearance of brain MR changes, short stature, and cortisol deficiency.

nodes.description: Brain MRI may show delayed myelination as part of the neurologic disease spectrum.

evidence.snippet: Six-month-old patient presented with cholestatic liver disease, macrocytic anemia, developmental delay, generalized hypotonia, delayed brain myelination, and elevated levels of serum methionine.
elevated concentration of S-adenosylhomocysteine.

readouts.interpretation: Elevated S-adenosylhomocysteine reflects disruption of the adenosine-sensitive transmethylation cycle.

evidence.snippet: methionine level in patients with ADK deficiency was strongly increased compared with patients with other liver diseases.
Biochemically, methionine is elevated with normal homocysteine levels and the diagnosis is confirmed through molecular analysis of the ADK gene.

readouts.interpretation: Higher plasma methionine reflects the ADK-related methionine-cycle disturbance.

nodes.description: Dysmorphic facial features are repeatedly reported as part of the human ADK deficiency phenotype.

evidence.snippet: It is characterized by developmental delay, hypotonia, epilepsy, facial dysmorphism, failure to thrive, transient liver dysfunction with cholestasis, recurrent hypoglycemia, and cardiac defects.

nodes.description: Poor weight gain is described in several affected infants.

evidence.snippet: failure to thrive

nodes.description: Affected children commonly have delayed psychomotor development and global developmental delay.

evidence.snippet: Adenosine kinase (ADK) deficiency is characterized by liver disease, dysmorphic features, epilepsy and developmental delay.
Subsequently, patients demonstrate hypotonia and global developmental delay.

nodes.description: Patients may present with neonatal jaundice, cholestasis, transaminase elevation, transient liver dysfunction, or even liver failure.

evidence.snippet: It is characterized by developmental delay, hypotonia, epilepsy, facial dysmorphism, failure to thrive, transient liver dysfunction with cholestasis, recurrent hypoglycemia, and cardiac defects.
Adenosine kinase (ADK) deficiency is characterized by liver disease, dysmorphic features, epilepsy and developmental delay.
Six-month-old patient presented with cholestatic liver disease, macrocytic anemia, developmental delay, generalized hypotonia, delayed brain myelination, and elevated levels of serum methionine.

nodes.description: Elevated plasma methionine is a recurring biochemical hallmark and is diagnostically useful in ADK deficiency.

evidence.snippet: methionine level in patients with ADK deficiency was strongly increased compared with patients with other liver diseases.
Biochemically, methionine is elevated with normal homocysteine levels and the diagnosis is confirmed through molecular analysis of the ADK gene.

nodes.description: Recurrent hypoglycemia is reported in affected children with the hepatic metabolic phenotype.

evidence.snippet: It is characterized by developmental delay, hypotonia, epilepsy, facial dysmorphism, failure to thrive, transient liver dysfunction with cholestasis, recurrent hypoglycemia, and cardiac defects.

nodes.description: Generalized hypotonia is commonly reported in the neonatal or infantile presentation.

evidence.snippet: It is characterized by developmental delay, hypotonia, epilepsy, facial dysmorphism, failure to thrive, transient liver dysfunction with cholestasis, recurrent hypoglycemia, and cardiac defects.
generalized hypotonia

nodes.description: Liver transplantation can correct the hepatic component, although neurologic disease may continue to progress.

evidence.snippet: The patient underwent living-donor liver transplantation (LTx) at 14 months of age. Ten-year follow-up after LTx revealed a preserved good liver function, persistent regenerative macrocytic anemia, progressive neurological disease but disappearance of brain MR changes, short stature, and cortisol deficiency.

nodes.description: Persistent macrocytic anemia has been documented in long-term follow-up.

evidence.snippet: Six-month-old patient presented with cholestatic liver disease, macrocytic anemia, developmental delay, generalized hypotonia, delayed brain myelination, and elevated levels of serum methionine.

nodes.description: Dietary methionine intake restriction has been tried, but outcomes are variable and it is not curative.

evidence.snippet: There is no curative treatment; however, a methionine-restricted diet has been tried with variable outcomes.

nodes.description: ADK deficiency can secondarily reduce mitochondrial respiratory-chain activity in liver or skeletal muscle, with reported combined liver complex defects and reduced complex II/III activity in skeletal muscle.

evidence.snippet: As the phenotype was consistent with a mitochondrial disorder, we studied liver mitochondrial respiratory chain activities in two patients and revealed a combined defect of several complexes.
A decrease of mitochondrial respiratory chain complex II and III activity were found in the postnuclear supernatants obtained from skeletal muscle biopsy.

nodes.description: ADK deficiency causes hypotonia, global developmental delay, epilepsy, and cognitive impairment; brain-specific deficiency can directly drive seizure susceptibility and learning deficits, and neurologic disease may persist despite liver transplantation.

evidence.snippet: We conclude that ADK deficiency in the brain triggers neuronal adaptation processes that lead to dysregulated synaptic plasticity, cognitive deficits, and increased seizure risk.

Adenosine kinase (ADK) deficiency is a very rare inborn error of methionine and adenosine metabolism. It is characterized by developmental delay, hypotonia, epilepsy, facial dysmorphism, failure to thrive, transient liver dysfunction with cholestasis, recurrent hypoglycemia, and cardiac defects.
Subsequently, patients demonstrate hypotonia and global developmental delay.
Biochemically, methionine is elevated with normal homocysteine levels and the diagnosis is confirmed through molecular analysis of the ADK gene.

readouts.interpretation: Normal homocysteine is part of the diagnostic biochemical signature when interpreted together with elevated methionine.

nodes.description: Epilepsy and seizures are recurrent neurologic manifestations and may be driven directly by brain ADK deficiency.

evidence.snippet: Similar to ADK-deficient patients, AdkΔbrain mice develop seizures and cognitive deficits.
epilepsy

nodes.description: Short stature was reported during long-term follow-up after liver transplantation in a patient later diagnosed with ADK deficiency.

evidence.snippet: Ten-year follow-up after LTx revealed a preserved good liver function, persistent regenerative macrocytic anemia, progressive neurological disease but disappearance of brain MR changes, short stature, and cortisol deficiency.

nodes.description: Stroke has been reported in ADK deficiency in association with tortuous cervical arteries.

evidence.snippet: vascular tortuosity leading to stroke in one of them.

nodes.description: Accumulation of adenosine and S-adenosylhomocysteine perturbs one-carbon metabolism, producing elevated methionine, elevated AdoMet/AdoHcy, and a biochemical pattern that is diagnostically useful.

evidence.snippet: In addition, we retrospectively analyzed methionine plasma concentration, a hallmark of ADK deficiency, in a cohort of children and showed that methionine level in patients with ADK deficiency was strongly increased compared with patients with other liver diseases.

Whole exome sequencing revealed the patient to be affected with two novel ADK variants, which pathogenicity was confirmed biochemically by demonstration of elevated concentration of S-adenosylhomocysteine.
Biochemically, methionine is elevated with normal homocysteine levels and the diagnosis is confirmed through molecular analysis of the ADK gene.
